title: MLIP Studio: An Open Platform for Interactive Benchmarking and Atomistic Simulations Using Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials
authors: Manas Sharma, Sudeep Punnathanam, Ananth Govind Rajan
abstract: Universal machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) are foundation AI models transforming atomistic simulations, but their practical use remains hindered by fragmented software ecosystems, dependency conflicts, and the lack of accessible benchmarking tools. These models approach first-principles density functional theory (DFT) accuracy at a fraction of the computational cost. We introduce MLIP Studio (available at https://mlipstudio.iisc.ac.in), an open and free platform that brings more than 60 universal MLIPs into a unified interactive interface for molecules and materials. The platform enables end-to-end MLIP-driven workflows, including property prediction, geometry optimization, vibrational and equation-of-state analysis, spin-state determination, custom model deployment, and high-throughput benchmarking against reference data. Automated parity plots and sortable error tables facilitate rapid identification of element-wise outliers and problematic data points. We demonstrate that MLIP-based pre-optimization can reduce subsequent DFT optimization effort by ~33$\times$. Additionally, the application enables benchmarking of computational performance. Through a comprehensive case study involving the 2D magnetic material CrCl$_3$ on a sapphire substrate, we show how cross-model comparisons of various properties and potential-energy landscapes can guide task-specific MLIP selection. Overall, MLIP Studio lowers the barrier to the reliable use of foundation models in end-to-end research workflows, benchmarking, and education in computational chemistry and materials science.

infographic.key_insight: MLIP Studio is an open platform that unifies 60+ universal machine learning interatomic potentials into one interactive workflow for benchmarking, simulation, and property prediction.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It makes MLIPs easier to compare, deploy, and trust for practical atomistic modeling, reducing dependence on fragmented software stacks and lowering the cost of DFT-like workflows.
method: Unified interface for molecules and materials
Supports property prediction, geometry optimization, vibrational and equation-of-state analysis, spin-state determination, and custom model deployment
Includes high-throughput benchmarking with parity plots, sortable error tables, and computational performance evaluation
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports that MLIP-based pre-optimization can cut subsequent DFT optimization effort by about 33× and includes a case study on CrCl3 on sapphire to compare model behavior.
infographic.watch_item: Benchmark results and model rankings may depend strongly on the specific task, reference data, and domain coverage, so transferability should be checked before use in new systems.
infographic.paper_title: MLIP Studio: An Open Platform for Interactive Benchmarking and Atomistic Simulations Using Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials

title: Machine Learning Materials Properties by Encoding Orbital-Projected Density of States
authors: Paulo Pires, Pierre-Paul De Breuck, Mauro Fava, Hai-Chen Wang, Miguel A. L. Marques
abstract: Graph neural networks have become the dominant machine-learning architecture for predicting materials properties from crystal structures. Yet the initialization of atomic node features has received comparatively little attention, and conventional approaches rely on static elemental descriptors that carry no information about the quantum-mechanical electronic environment of each atom in its crystalline host. Here we show that augmenting atomic node representations with site-projected orbital density of states (pDOS) fingerprints, computed directly from density functional theory calculations, yields systematic and substantial improvements in predictive performance.These representations are fused with Pettifor elemental embeddings at each atomic site before message passing. For the superconducting critical temperature $T_c$ and the optical dielectric constant $ε_{\infty}$,the pDOS augmentation reduces prediction errors by 22.9% and 27.9%, respectively, relative to the elemental-descriptor baseline. These improvements are comparable to those achieved by doubling the training-set size. The gains are, however, contingent on training-set size. For the magnetic exchange energies of Heusler compounds, a substantially smaller dataset, the improvement is reduced,indicating that pDOS augmentation is most effective when the training data exceeds the length of the pDOS feature vector. We introduce an interpretable spectral attention-gating mechanism that reveals that the model autonomously learns to prioritize the orbital channels and energy windows most physically relevant to each target property. These results establish pDOS-augmented graph nodes as a broadly applicable strategy for infusing first-principles electronic-structure knowledge into graph networks, opening a practical route to high-accuracy property prediction in data-scarce regimes.

infographic.key_insight: Augmenting graph neural network atom features with site-projected density of states from DFT captures local electronic structure and significantly improves materials property prediction.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This offers a practical way to build more accurate property predictors for materials discovery by combining first-principles electronic information with crystal-graph learning.
method: Compute orbital-projected density of states (pDOS) fingerprints for each atomic site from DFT.
Fuse pDOS features with Pettifor elemental embeddings before message passing in a graph neural network.
Use a spectral attention-gating mechanism to identify which orbitals and energy ranges matter most for each target property.
infographic.evidence_signal: Compared with an elemental-descriptor baseline, pDOS augmentation reduced errors by 22.9% for superconducting Tc and 27.9% for optical dielectric constant ε∞, with gains comparable to doubling the training set.
infographic.watch_item: The benefit depends on dataset size and is weaker when the training set is smaller than the pDOS feature dimension, so the added electronic descriptors may not help equally in data-scarce cases.
infographic.paper_title: Machine Learning Materials Properties by Encoding Orbital-Projected Density of States

title: A Multi-Scale Machine Learning Framework for Coupled Chemical, Spin, and Structural Disorder in Alloys
authors: Zhenyao Fang, Qimin Yan
abstract: Understanding the thermodynamic properties of disordered magnetic alloys requires a unified treatment of configurational (chemical, spin, etc.) and structural degrees of freedom, which has remained beyond the scope of existing computational frameworks. Here we present a general framework that integrates machine learning models (such as graph neural networks and machine learning interatomic potentials) and statistical sampling methods (such as Monte Carlo and molecular dynamics simulations) to study the coupled chemical, spin, and structural disorder in alloys. We demonstrate the framework on body-centered-cubic Fe-Co alloys with interstitial carbon dopants, where the Fe-Co host exhibits intrinsic chemical and spin disorder, and the interstitial carbon introduces additional structural disorder through local lattice distortions, making the system a prototypical multi-disorder magnetic alloy. The framework predicts the order-to-disorder phase transition temperature and the melting temperature of Fe-Co alloy to be 1,000 K and 1,690 K, in excellent agreement with the experimentally measured values of 1,006 K and approximately 1,700 K, respectively. It also predicts the tetragonal-to-nearly-cubic structural transitions in Fe-Co-C alloy as temperature increases. These results establish the framework as a reliable tool for studying multi-disorder alloys, with applications to complex disordered systems such as high-entropy alloys, multiferroics, and spintronic devices.

infographic.key_insight: A multi-scale ML framework can jointly model chemical, spin, and structural disorder in alloys to predict phase behavior across temperature.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable design and screening of complex magnetic and high-entropy alloys where multiple coupled disorders control performance.
method: Combines graph neural networks and machine-learning interatomic potentials.
Uses Monte Carlo and molecular dynamics to sample configurational and structural disorder.
Applies the workflow to bcc Fe-Co and Fe-Co-C as a prototype multi-disorder alloy system.
infographic.evidence_signal: The framework reproduces key experimental temperatures for Fe-Co, predicting the order-disorder transition at 1,000 K and melting at 1,690 K versus measured values of 1,006 K and about 1,700 K.
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on accurate training data and efficient coupling of ML models with sampling, which may be challenging for alloys with even more complex disorder landscapes.
infographic.paper_title: A Multi-Scale Machine Learning Framework for Coupled Chemical, Spin, and Structural Disorder in Alloys

title: PhononScore: a phonon-aware scoring function for dynamical stability
authors: Xiao-Qi Han, Ze-Feng Gao, Zhong-Yi Lu
abstract: In recent years, crystal generation models have enabled the design of massive numbers of candidate materials. However, the lack of dynamical stability among generated structures has become a major bottleneck preventing their translation into practical materials discovery. To address this challenge, we propose PhononScore, a phonon-aware scoring function for crystal generation. Unlike computationally expensive explicit phonon calculations, PhononScore predicts a unified stability score from crystal structures, enabling ranking of candidate materials dynamical stability with second-level computational cost. We construct a multi-fidelity phonon dataset containing 157,463 crystal structures. On the PhononBench benchmark, PhononScore improves the average dynamical stability rate of candidate pools generated by nine crystal generation models from 30.7% to 83.7%, achieving a 2.72-fold enrichment of stable structures, while the average stability rate of the Top-10 candidates reaches 97.5%. On a high-fidelity DFT-PBE phonon benchmark, the DFT-finetuned PhononScore-DFT increases the Top-100 stability rate to 93.0% and achieves 5-6-fold enrichment of dynamically stable structures under an extremely imbalanced hard-screening scenario. As a materials-screening tool analogous to scoring functions in drug discovery, PhononScore can serve directly as a dynamical-stability feedback signal for crystal generation, active learning, and reinforcement learning, enabling second-level stability-aware reranking without explicit phonon calculations and providing a unified and efficient dynamical stability evaluator for high-throughput materials discovery, active learning, reinforcement learning, and closed-loop inverse design. The online PhononScore platform is available at: http://phononbench.cn/phononscore/

infographic.key_insight: PhononScore is a fast, phonon-aware scoring function that ranks crystal candidates by dynamical stability without explicit phonon calculations.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It gives materials-discovery pipelines a second-level, stability-aware filter for reranking, active learning, and reinforcement learning in high-throughput inverse design.
method: Trains a unified stability scorer on a multi-fidelity phonon dataset of 157,463 crystal structures.
Predicts dynamical stability directly from crystal structures instead of running expensive phonon computations.
Provides a feedback signal for crystal generation model reranking and can be finetuned to DFT-quality phonon data.
infographic.evidence_signal: On PhononBench, it raised average stability from 30.7% to 83.7% across nine generators, and Top-10 stability reached 97.5%; the DFT-finetuned version reached 93.0% Top-100 stability on a high-fidelity benchmark.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on benchmark coverage and training fidelity, so extrapolation to out-of-distribution chemistries or structures may still require explicit phonon validation.
infographic.paper_title: PhononScore: a phonon-aware scoring function for dynamical stability

title: Human and LLM Collaboration for Accelerated Materials Synthesis and Discovery
authors: Gregory Bassen, Wyatt Bunstine, Sarah Okandey, Sarah Cheung, Elaine Flowers, Ritwik Bose
abstract: Although Large Language Models (LLM) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools have enabled a rapid increase in the generation rate of predicted materials, the rate of new materials discovery has lagged behind. This is due to the challenges associated with designing a sequence of chemical reactions to predictably produce new materials, especially in new structure types. Here, we report a study of human and LLM generated recipes for the synthesis of known and new materials. The success of the recipes is determined through in-lab experimentation, and the results are passed back to the humans and LLMs in a closed-loop process to study the effects of their collaboration. The Ruddlesden-Popper homologous series was selected for all material candidates to provide a materials phase space that is simultaneously well studied and likely to host undiscovered materials. We find that humans (H) and LLM (L) have similar success rates: 83(8)% (H) and 75(9)% (L) [known materials, round one], 17(9)% (H) and 22(10)% (L) [unknown materials, round one], 79(8)% (H) and 71(9)% (L) [known materials, round two], and 22(7)% (H) and 14(6)% (L) [unknown materials, round two]. Through this collaborative human-LLM effort, we discovered Ba3PtO5, a material with a new structural prototype that constitutes the missing 1D member of the herein reported dimensionally tunable Rock-Salt Perovskite (RSP) homologous series of the form (AX)m(ABX3)p, of which the Ruddlesden-Popper series is a subset.

infographic.key_insight: Human and LLM collaboration achieved comparable recipe-generation success, and the closed-loop process helped discover a new material prototype, Ba3PtO5, in the Ruddlesden-Popper-related phase space.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This work shows that pairing LLMs with experimental feedback can accelerate synthesis planning for materials discovery, especially when exploring underrepresented structure types.
method: Generate synthesis recipes for known and candidate new materials using humans and LLMs
Test recipe success through in-lab experimentation
Feed results back into a closed-loop collaboration and repeat across rounds
infographic.evidence_signal: Across two rounds, success rates were similar for humans and LLMs on known materials but much lower for unknown materials, with the reported discovery of Ba3PtO5 validating the approach.
infographic.watch_item: Performance drops sharply for unknown targets, so experimental throughput and error control remain critical when translating LLM-generated recipes to the lab.
infographic.paper_title: Human and LLM Collaboration for Accelerated Materials Synthesis and Discovery

title: EquiFiLM: Charge-Conditioned Equivariant Force Fields via Feature-wise Linear Modulation
authors: Samuel Sahel-Schackis, Ken-ichi Nomura, Aiichiro Nakano, Matthias F. Kling, Thomas Linker
abstract: Foundation machine learning force fields (MLFFs) such as MACE-MP-0 and UMA cover broad chemical space at near density functional theory (DFT) accuracy. However, they assume equilibrium ground-state physics and do not natively handle externally induced changes to the electronic state, such as charging, applied fields, or electronic excitation, which limits their use for driven processes such as photoexcitation and charge injection. We propose EquiFiLM, a lightweight extension that adds continuous external conditioning to any equivariant foundation MLFF via a per-layer Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) block, learning externally driven changes to the potential energy surface from minimal training data. The block modulates only scalar channels and preserves E(3)-equivariance exactly. We demonstrate the recipe on charged liquid water with the foundation model MACE-MatPES as the backbone, yielding E-MACE. On the four training charges, E-MACE delivers a $3.1\times$ reduction in force RMSE ($21.3$ to $6.96$ meV/$\mathring{A}$) and a $61\times$ reduction in per-atom energy RMSE ($6.1$ to $0.1$ meV/atom) over a baseline without EquiFiLM trained on the same data, at indistinguishable inference cost. Across seven held-out interpolation and extrapolation charges, force RMSE stays within $18-61$ meV/$\mathring{A}$ and energy RMSE within $0.7-5.4$ meV/atom. The model runs stable molecular dynamics across the full range tested and predicts the charge-dependent first-shell response of the reduced pair distribution function probed by ultrafast electron diffraction. Adding this conditioning axis to the foundation requires only a few thousand DFT-labeled frames, against the $\approx 10^8$ structures of a charge-aware foundation trained from scratch. The recipe is backbone- and conditioning-agnostic: it applies without architectural change to any equivariant MLFF with scalar interaction-layer channels.

infographic.key_insight: EquiFiLM adds charge or other external conditioning to equivariant foundation force fields with a simple FiLM block on scalar channels, enabling accurate, low-data modeling of driven electronic-state changes without breaking E(3) symmetry.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This makes pretrained ML force fields usable for charged, excited, or field-driven simulations in catalysis, electrochemistry, and ultrafast dynamics at near-DFT fidelity and with negligible extra inference cost.
method: Insert per-layer FiLM modulation into an equivariant backbone, conditioning only scalar features so symmetry is preserved exactly.
Train on a small set of DFT-labeled frames for the target external condition, then interpolate and extrapolate across unseen condition values.
Demonstrate the recipe on charged liquid water using MACE-MatPES as the backbone, producing E-MACE.
infographic.evidence_signal: On four training charges, E-MACE reduced force RMSE from 21.3 to 6.96 meV/Å and energy RMSE from 6.1 to 0.1 meV/atom, and remained stable across seven held-out charges with force RMSE of 18–61 meV/Å.
infographic.watch_item: Performance still depends on having representative labeled data across the conditioning range, and extrapolation beyond the tested charge domain may be less reliable.
infographic.paper_title: EquiFiLM: Charge-Conditioned Equivariant Force Fields via Feature-wise Linear Modulation

title: BatteryMat: a hierarchical machine-learning and DFT framework for average-voltage screening of lithium-ion cathode materials
authors: Jaehyung Lee, Charles Rhys Campbell, Kent Zhang, Kamal Choudhary
abstract: Density functional theory (DFT) predicts cathode voltages accurately but does not scale to the combinatorial chemical spaces of modern materials databases, while pure machine-learning surrogates are fast but cannot guarantee thermodynamic consistency. We introduce BatteryMat, a three-tier framework that promotes single-pass average-voltage prediction with the Atomistic Line Graph Neural Network (ALIGNN) as the primary screening signal across JARVIS-DFT, then validates survivors with ALIGNN-FF force-field delithiation profiles and automated PBE+U or optB88-vdW+U supercell DFT. The exchange-correlation functional is selected automatically by spacegroup, and the lithium metal reference is recomputed in the same plane-wave basis as the cathode runs, removing a systematic offset of about 1 V present in tabulated values. Trained on 7,610 ALIGNN-FF delithiation voltages, the ALIGNN predictor reproduces the force-field labels with a mean absolute error of 0.17 V and a coefficient of determination of 0.94; this measures distillation fidelity to the force-field protocol, not agreement with DFT or experiment. On four commercial chemistries (LiFePO4, LiMnPO4, LiMn2O4, LiCoO2) the DFT tier reproduces the experimental average voltage to within 0.3 V and the theoretical volumetric capacity to within 5%; a fifth, non-stoichiometric layered entry is carried as an edge case. The pipeline prioritises, rather than generates, existing structures: it ranks the lithium-containing JARVIS-DFT pool into 71 candidates and a scan of about 4.49 million Alexandria structures into 213, all surrogate-level leads awaiting DFT validation rather than confirmed cathodes. BatteryMat is available at https://github.com/atomgptlab/batterymat with a demo at https://atomgpt.org/battery.

infographic.key_insight: BatteryMat combines fast machine-learning screening with DFT validation to rank lithium-ion cathode candidates by average voltage while preserving thermodynamic consistency.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The framework can accelerate cathode discovery across large materials databases by narrowing millions of structures to a small set of DFT-ready leads.
method: Tier 1: ALIGNN predicts average voltage as a rapid surrogate screen over JARVIS-DFT and Alexandria structures.
Tier 2: ALIGNN-FF delithiation profiles validate survivors using force-field-based voltage traces.
Tier 3: automated supercell DFT with PBE+U or optB88-vdW+U finalizes voltage and capacity estimates, with the functional chosen by spacegroup.
infographic.evidence_signal: ALIGNN-FF training on 7,610 delithiation voltages achieved MAE 0.17 V and R² = 0.94, and the DFT tier matched four commercial cathodes within about 0.3 V of experimental average voltage.
infographic.watch_item: The ML model is only distilling the force-field protocol and the pipeline prioritizes existing structures, so surrogate hits still require careful DFT validation before being treated as confirmed cathodes.
infographic.paper_title: BatteryMat: a hierarchical machine-learning and DFT framework for average-voltage screening of lithium-ion cathode materials

title: Interplay between Electronic Structure, Chemical Bonding, and Lattice Symmetry in Bismuth Vanadate
authors: Philip Schwinghammer, Franziska S. Hegner, Frederico P. Delgado, Michel Panhans, Konrad Merkel, Frank Ortmann
abstract: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO$_4$) is a prototypical oxide photocatalyst that occurs in both tetragonal and monoclinic scheelite phases with markedly different photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical activities. Accurately identifying the monoclinic phase as the ground state and explaining the origin of its symmetry-breaking distortion are unusually challenging from a theoretical perspective, with various levels of theory and associated physical interpretations for this behaviour reported in the literature. Here, we resolve these discrepancies by systematically assessing the role of exact exchange with and without spin-orbit coupling, demonstrating that an accurate treatment of electronic localization is essential to stabilize the monoclinic scheelite structure. Using this framework, we compute the electronic band structure through dense sampling of the Brillouin zone and show that the band edges in monoclinic and tetragonal BiVO$_4$ lie far from conventional high-symmetry paths, leading to substantial differences in band gaps and carrier effective masses. Choosing the exchange-correlation functional that best reproduces the crystal structure leads to excellent predictions of the band gap once excitonic and thermal effects are taken into account. In addition, we show that the monoclinic distortion is driven by charge transfer between non-equivalent oxygen sites, which breaks the lattice symmetry and is suppressed by self-interaction errors when using semi-local DFT. These results establish a direct connection between the exchange-correlation functional, electronic localization, chemical bonding, and structural stability in BiVO$_4$, providing a foundation for robust ab initio descriptions of phase stability and optoelectronic properties in such complex oxides.

infographic.key_insight: Accurate inclusion of exact exchange is essential to capture electronic localization, which stabilizes monoclinic BiVO4 and explains its symmetry-breaking distortion and optoelectronic behavior.
infographic.engineering_relevance: Selecting the right exchange-correlation functional can materially improve phase stability prediction and band-gap estimates for oxide photocatalysts and related functional materials.
method: Compared DFT treatments with and without exact exchange and spin-orbit coupling.
Computed dense Brillouin-zone band structures for monoclinic and tetragonal BiVO4.
Linked structural distortion to charge transfer between inequivalent oxygen sites and electronic localization.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports systematic comparison across theory levels, dense band-structure sampling, and agreement with the crystal structure and band gap once excitonic and thermal effects are included.
infographic.watch_item: Results depend strongly on the chosen exchange-correlation functional, so semi-local DFT may miss the monoclinic ground state and distort predicted electronic properties.
infographic.paper_title: Interplay between Electronic Structure, Chemical Bonding, and Lattice Symmetry in Bismuth Vanadate

title: Bond, orbital and spin order in d4/d6/d7 perovskite oxides: successes and limitations of foundation interatomic potentials
authors: Swagata Acharya, Dimitar Pashov, Mark van Schilfgaarde, Alin M. Elena
abstract: Foundation machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) are rapidly replacing density-functional theory (DFT) for modeling structure and nuclear dynamics, making their fidelity in strongly correlated systems an urgent question. We test three foundation potentials on the low-temperature order of three correlated, isostructural ABO3 perovskite oxides: LaMnO3 (d4), LaCoO3 (d6), and NdNiO3 (d7). We run molecular dynamics for 1 ns on 80- and 160-atom supercells from 50 to 300 K with no system-specific training. These oxides expose three distinct classes of low-temperature order that define a hierarchy of difficulty for the potentials. The scalar class, represented by NdNiO3, has a simple geometric fingerprint and is captured. The vector class, represented by LaMnO3, requires identifying which Cartesian axis carries the long bond at each site, and is captured in magnitude but not in symmetry. The on-site class, represented by the low-spin to high-spin crossover in LaCoO3, is a purely local multiplet population shift with no spatial order parameter and remains inaccessible to present-day MLIPs.

infographic.key_insight: Foundation ML interatomic potentials can reproduce some low-temperature ordering in correlated perovskite oxides, but their success depends strongly on whether the order is scalar, vector, or purely on-site in character.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For computational materials design, this work shows where foundation potentials are reliable replacements for DFT in oxide structure/dynamics and where correlated-electron physics still requires caution or higher-fidelity methods.
method: Tested three foundation MLIPs on LaMnO3, LaCoO3, and NdNiO3 without system-specific training.
Ran 1 ns molecular dynamics on 80- and 160-atom supercells across 50–300 K.
Compared whether each model recovered the expected low-temperature bond, orbital, and spin order.
infographic.evidence_signal: The evaluation spans three isostructural ABO3 oxides with distinct order types, and the abstract reports that NdNiO3 is captured, LaMnO3 is partially captured, and LaCoO3 remains inaccessible to current models.
infographic.watch_item: These results indicate that foundation MLIPs may miss symmetry-specific or purely local spin-state transitions, so validation against correlated-electron order is essential before deployment.
infographic.paper_title: Bond, orbital and spin order in d4/d6/d7 perovskite oxides: successes and limitations of foundation interatomic potentials

title: Accurate, Interdisciplinary and Transparent Structure-property Understanding with Deep Native Structural Reasoning
authors: Chen Tang, Yizhou Wang, Jianyu Wu, Lintao Wang, Shixiang Tang, Pengze Li
abstract: Structure-property relationships are foundational to biology, chemistry and materials science, where function, reactivity and physical response emerge from spatial, chemical and periodic organization. Mechanistically explaining these relationships requires interpreting structural evidence through scientific principles and physical constraints, from stereochemistry and bonding to symmetry, energetics and periodic order. However, applying artificial intelligence to this process presents a joint challenge of representation and reasoning: models must preserve domain-native structural information while showing how specific evidence supports predictions under these constraints. Here we introduce SciReasoner, a multimodal scientific foundation model for native structural reasoning across proteins, small molecules and inorganic crystals. SciReasoner discretizes coordinates, topologies and periodic connectivities into a unified structure-aware vocabulary, treating structural tokens as addressable evidence units during reasoning. In homology-controlled Gene Ontology prediction, SciReasoner improves Cellular Component annotation for low-homology and orphan-like proteins, increasing $F_{\max}$ from 0.42 to 0.55. In chemistry, it raises single-step retrosynthesis accuracy from 0.63 to 0.72 while generating fragment-level disconnection and precursor-verification traces. In materials science, its representations separate elemental and compound phases and resolve high- and low-band-gap regimes. Across 86 benchmarks, SciReasoner achieves state-of-the-art performance on 67 tasks. Double-blind expert evaluation rates its reasoning traces as preferred or at least comparable to those of a frontier large language model in 98% of cases. By making structure an inspectable substrate for reasoning under scientific constraints, SciReasoner connects accurate prediction with interpretable scientific inference.

infographic.key_insight: SciReasoner turns structural data into addressable tokens so a multimodal model can predict and explain structure-property relationships across proteins, molecules, and crystals.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The approach could improve materials and molecular design workflows by combining higher prediction accuracy with inspectable reasoning traces under scientific constraints.
method: Discretizes coordinates, topologies, and periodic connectivities into a unified structure-aware vocabulary.
Uses structural tokens as evidence units for native reasoning across biology, chemistry, and materials.
Learns representations that separate phases and capture regime changes such as band-gap differences.
infographic.evidence_signal: Across 86 benchmarks, the model reports state-of-the-art results on 67 tasks, including Fmax improving from 0.42 to 0.55 on low-homology/orphan-like Gene Ontology Cellular Component prediction and retrosynthesis accuracy from 0.63 to 0.72.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on high-quality structural inputs and careful validation that the generated reasoning traces are faithful rather than merely plausible.
infographic.paper_title: Accurate, Interdisciplinary and Transparent Structure-property Understanding with Deep Native Structural Reasoning

title: A Distributional Framework for Generative Modeling of Molecular Crystals
authors: Michael Kilgour, Alex Dong, Mark E. Tuckerman, Jutta Rogal
abstract: Molecular crystals are a highly polymorphic class of materials, with a single molecule commonly crystallizing via multiple packing patterns, making structure and property prediction very challenging. Crystal structure prediction typically comprises the production of sets of promising candidate structures, each considered in isolation rather than as samples in a thermodynamic distribution. Likewise, modern generative approaches to this problem, despite naturally sampling distributions of crystals, lack a concrete formulation of the distributions being sampled. Two components are required to impart meaning to the distributions of crystals generated under such models: a canonical parameterization, and a loss function which equilibrates the generated samples to some target distribution. We develop such a parameterization, and train energy-based generative flow networks (GFlowNets) to approximate the Boltzmann distribution over crystal structures for target molecules and space groups. Combined, these components comprise our MXtalGFlow framework for molecular crystal modeling. Going beyond sampling disconnected sets of low-energy structures, MXtalGFlow yields a thermodynamic distribution over crystal structures. We sample and analyze distributions of crystals for two molecules, each under two energy functions, a Lennard-Jones potential and the Universal Model for Atoms. We characterize the local structural basins about the known polymorphs, and identify additional as-yet un-reported packing modes with competitive probabilities to the known experimental structures. With MXtalGFlow, we illustrate how to define and train a model to sample a thermodynamically meaningful distribution of molecular crystals, and analyze such a distribution to glean useful information.

infographic.key_insight: MXtalGFlow turns molecular crystal generation into sampling a thermodynamically meaningful Boltzmann distribution, rather than producing isolated candidate structures.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This provides a principled way to explore polymorphism and prioritize experimentally relevant crystal packings for materials discovery and structure prediction.
method: Defines a canonical crystal parameterization for molecular crystals and space groups.
Trains energy-based Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) to match a target Boltzmann distribution over crystal structures.
Analyzes sampled distributions to identify local structural basins and competitive alternative packing modes.
infographic.evidence_signal: The framework is evaluated on two molecules under two energy models (Lennard-Jones and the Universal Model for Atoms), recovering known polymorph basins and additional plausible packing modes.
infographic.watch_item: Results depend on the chosen energy function and space-group/parameterization choices, so distribution quality may be sensitive to modeling assumptions.
infographic.paper_title: A Distributional Framework for Generative Modeling of Molecular Crystals

title: Stress calculation in linear scaling DFT: convergence and dynamics
authors: Shereif Y. Mujahed, Tsuyoshi Miyazaki, David R. Bowler
abstract: We present the approach needed to calculate stress within density functional theory (DFT) using a localised orbital basis, both for exact diagonalisation and linear scaling approaches, and demonstrate our implementation within the large scale DFT code Conquest. For the linear scaling approach, we test the rate of convergence of stress with density matrix range, and compare it to the convergence of energy and forces for different materials with a range of band gaps. We show that excellent convergence is found for modest cutoffs, and show that large-scale isothermal-isobaric molecular dynamics is stable and accurate.

infographic.key_insight: The paper shows that stress can be computed accurately in localized-orbital DFT, including linear-scaling methods, with good convergence at modest density-matrix cutoffs.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables reliable stress-driven simulations such as pressure control and large-scale isothermal-isobaric molecular dynamics in computational materials design.
method: Derived stress expressions for DFT with localized orbitals.
Implemented the approach in the large-scale DFT code Conquest.
Tested stress convergence versus density-matrix range across materials with different band gaps.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports comparison of stress convergence against energy and forces, and states that large-scale isothermal-isobaric molecular dynamics was stable and accurate.
infographic.watch_item: Accuracy depends on choosing a sufficiently large density-matrix cutoff, so overly aggressive truncation may degrade stress quality.
infographic.paper_title: Stress calculation in linear scaling DFT: convergence and dynamics

title: Reaction-network reasoning with frontier models for experimentally confirmed catalyst-selectivity hypotheses
authors: Sutanay Choudhury, Anwesha Banerjee, Udishnu Sanyal, Jorin Dawidowicz, Chiezugolum Ijeoma Odilinye, Jesun Firoz
abstract: Catalysts are essential for sustainable chemical manufacturing, yet discovering novel architectures remains a bottleneck dominated by trial-and-error experimentation and computationally intensive screening. In complex reactions such as electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction, product selectivity is governed by dynamic interfacial, electrolyte, and potential factors as well as kinetic pathway competition. Conventional descriptor-based machine learning and computational potentials struggle to resolve these mechanistic branch points, primarily relying on static ground-state descriptors or bulk structural correlations rather than end-to-end topological pathway analysis. Here, we show that frontier language models, when strictly constrained to reason over explicit reaction networks, can discover novel catalysts by identifying the physical levers that govern pathway competition. We developed a human-AI co-thinking framework that enforces network invariance to extract testable hypotheses from complex chemical graphs. Applied to CO2 electroreduction, the framework identified ketene desorption and hydroxide capture as the acetate-forming pathway, and predicted a distinct adsorbed CO and CH2 coupling route to ketene. By isolating actionable control levers, specifically local alkalinity, controlled iron incorporation, and restricted interfacial proton-donor accessibility, the framework guided the prospective synthesis of a copper-iron oxide catalyst demonstrating a threefold increase in acetate selectivity over matched Cu-rich baselines. This mechanism-guided reasoning architecture shifts the computational paradigm from retrospective statistical prediction to forward-looking hypothesis generation, providing a broadly applicable blueprint for mechanism-guided materials discovery.

infographic.key_insight: A frontier language model constrained to explicit reaction-network reasoning can generate experimentally testable catalyst-selectivity hypotheses and reveal controllable levers for CO2 electroreduction.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables mechanism-guided catalyst design that can steer product selectivity, demonstrated by a copper-iron oxide catalyst with about 3× higher acetate selectivity than Cu-rich baselines.
method: Enforced network invariance so the model reasons over reaction graphs rather than static descriptors.
Mapped pathway competition in CO2 electroreduction to identify ketene desorption, hydroxide capture, and adsorbed CO/CH2 coupling routes.
Translated the inferred levers into synthesis guidance: local alkalinity, iron incorporation, and limited proton-donor access.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports prospective experimental validation, where the model-guided copper-iron oxide catalyst achieved a threefold increase in acetate selectivity versus matched Cu-rich controls.
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on accurate reaction-network specification and careful constraint design, so incomplete mechanistic graphs could lead to misleading hypotheses.
infographic.paper_title: Reaction-network reasoning with frontier models for experimentally confirmed catalyst-selectivity hypotheses

title: The Radial Distribution Functions of Nanofluids: Molecular Dynamics Simulations
authors: Özlem Öztürk
abstract: Nanofluids, which are composed of insoluble, stable, and well-dispersed solid particles of nanoscale and/or subnanometer sizes suspended in a base liquid, are the next generation of liquids of today. The purpose of this paper is to investigate the one dimensional and three dimensional angle dependent radial distribution functions RDF and ARDF of polymeric nanofluids made up of nonrigid (soft) nanoparticles and a polymer melt (base fluid) using the molecular dynamics simulation approach and to search the shape stabilities by using these results. For this purpose, we use the nanoparticles of three different sizes: 28, 42, and 56 particles. We research them both within the base fluid and without this polymeric medium for instability analysis. We found that the nanoparticles with 28 atoms show the shape instability inside the base fluid when we increase the system temperature from T=1.2 to T=1.8 and hence, the structure of two concentric spherical shell of the nanoparticle breaks down and as a result the empty vacuum between these inner and the outer shells disappears. In contrast to this findings, the nanoparticles with 42 and 56 atoms show the shape stability inside the base fluid by preserving their concentric shell structures when we rise the system temperature and decrease the affinity between the nanoparticles and the base liquid medium.

infographic.key_insight: Molecular dynamics shows that nanofluid nanoparticle shape stability depends strongly on particle size and temperature, with 28-atom particles becoming unstable while 42- and 56-atom particles remain shell-stable.
infographic.engineering_relevance: These results help guide the selection of nanoparticle size and operating temperature for designing thermally robust polymeric nanofluids with stable structure.
method: Ran molecular dynamics simulations of polymeric nanofluids with nonrigid nanoparticles in a polymer melt.
Computed one-dimensional radial distribution functions (RDF) and three-dimensional angle-dependent RDFs (ARDF).
Compared nanoparticle behavior with and without the base fluid across temperatures from T = 1.2 to 1.8.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports temperature-dependent structural changes: the 28-atom nanoparticle loses its concentric shell structure in the polymer base fluid, while the 42- and 56-atom nanoparticles preserve it.
infographic.watch_item: The findings are based on simulated nanoscale systems and may not transfer directly to real fluids without experimental validation and sensitivity checks on interaction parameters.
infographic.paper_title: The Radial Distribution Functions of Nanofluids: Molecular Dynamics Simulations

title: Polycrystalline ferroelectric croconic acid for multisource environmental energy harvesting
authors: Gloria P. Moreno-Martínez, Hari K. Mishra, Xabier García-Casas, María Alcaire, Vanda Godinho, Juan R. Sanchez-Valencia
abstract: The development of organic ferroelectric materials through scalable and simplified fabrication routes remains a major challenge for next-generation energy-harvesting technologies. Here, polycrystalline croconic acid (CA) thin films are fabricated by vacuum sublimation onto Ar plasma-treated flexible substrates and stabilized by in situ encapsulation with an adamantane-based remote plasma polymer. This solvent-free strategy effectively suppresses surface degradation under ambient conditions, providing long-term stability. Piezoresponse force microscopy confirms robust ferroelectricity with an oblique polarization orientation, well-defined domains, and low nanoscale coercive fields. The films were integrated into multilayer piezoelectric and pyroelectric devices. The piezoelectric performance strongly depends on film thickness, while embedding the CA layer between dielectric polymeric films significantly improves the macroscopic response, reaching power densities of up to 37 microW m-2 for ca. 2 micrometer CA films. Despite the common assumption that high crystallinity is required to sustain ferro-, piezo-, and pyroelectricity, these polycrystalline CA films exhibit remarkable RT pyroelectricity, a property not previously demonstrated in CA-based devices. A pyroelectric coefficient of ca. 10 microC m-2 K-1 highlights a functional response comparable to that of well-established organic and inorganic pyroelectric materials, demonstrating the potential of CA thin films for thermal energy harvesting. Beyond their functional performance, the proposed low-T fabrication route combines deposition and encapsulation in a single in situ process, simplifying device fabrication. Its compatibility with scalable vacuum technologies, flexible substrates, and further process optimization makes this approach highly promising for developing low-cost, lead-free, multisource energy-harvesting systems.

infographic.key_insight: Polycrystalline croconic acid thin films can be vacuum-deposited and in situ encapsulated to deliver stable ferroelectric, piezoelectric, and pyroelectric energy-harvesting performance in a scalable organic device platform.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This low-temperature, solvent-free route is compatible with flexible substrates and vacuum processing, making it attractive for low-cost, lead-free multisource energy-harvesting devices.
method: Vacuum-sublimed croconic acid thin films were deposited on Ar plasma-treated flexible substrates.
An adamantane-based remote plasma polymer was applied in situ as an encapsulation layer to improve ambient stability.
The films were integrated into multilayer piezoelectric and pyroelectric device architectures.
infographic.evidence_signal: Performance was verified by PFM and device testing, including a maximum power density of about 37 microW m-2 for ~2 micrometer films and a pyroelectric coefficient of about 10 microC m-2 K-1.
infographic.watch_item: Device response depends strongly on film thickness, so reproducible thickness control and long-term encapsulation quality will be critical for practical deployment.
infographic.paper_title: Polycrystalline ferroelectric croconic acid for multisource environmental energy harvesting

title: Large-scale first-principle simulations of amorphous indium oxide
authors: Matthew Bousquet, Francois Gygi, Giulia Galli
abstract: Amorphous indium oxide (a-In$_2$O$_3$) is a high-electron-mobility semiconductor of central importance in thin-film transistors and a promising photoanode for solar-driven water oxidation. Despite sustained experimental and computational investigations, the structural motifs underlying its unusual transport properties and the existence of O-O peroxide-like bonds within its network have remained unresolved. Here we develop a MACE-based machine-learned interatomic potential trained on first-principles molecular dynamics trajectories and use it to generate and analyze amorphous structures containing up to 5120 atoms, two orders of magnitude larger than those adopted in typical ab initio studies. We find X-ray structure factors in excellent quantitative agreement with experiment and we confirm that In$_2$O$_3$ is a poor glass former, with the likely presence of quasi-crystalline regions in amorphous samples. Our large-scale structural analysis reveals extended chains of edge-sharing InO$_k$ polyhedra providing a concrete structural basis for the high electron mobility of a-In$_2$O$_3$. Our results strongly support the formation of O-O peroxide-like bonds in the amorphous network, with a mean length of 1.5 Å. We show that these bonds introduce localized in-gap states near the conduction band minimum, acting as a source of intrinsic n-type self-doping and enhancing sub-gap optical absorption. These effects are detectable via a distinct Raman feature near 850 cm$^{-1}$ that is absent in the IR spectrum. Overall, our results establish a comprehensive structure-property picture of a-In$_2$O$_3$, provide directly testable experimental predictions, and suggest that controlled amorphization is a viable strategy for improving the photoelectrochemical activity of a-In$_2$O$_3$.

infographic.key_insight: Large-scale machine-learned simulations reveal that amorphous indium oxide contains quasi-crystalline edge-sharing polyhedral chains and O-O peroxide-like bonds that explain its high mobility and intrinsic n-type behavior.
infographic.engineering_relevance: These structural insights can guide the design of amorphous In2O3 thin films and photoanodes with improved electron transport and optoelectronic performance.
method: MACE machine-learned interatomic potential trained on first-principles molecular dynamics
Large-scale amorphous structure generation and analysis up to 5120 atoms
Comparison of computed X-ray structure factors, Raman signatures, and electronic structure with experiment
infographic.evidence_signal: The simulations achieve excellent quantitative agreement with experimental X-ray structure factors and predict a distinct Raman feature near 850 cm^-1 linked to peroxide-like bonds.
infographic.watch_item: The conclusions rely on a trained interatomic potential, so transferability to other compositions, defect levels, or processing conditions should be validated carefully.
infographic.paper_title: Large-scale first-principle simulations of amorphous indium oxide

title: Layer-resolved Electronic Structure and Correlation of Low-$n$ Square-planar Nickelates: A DFT+DMFT Prediction of Superconducting Candidates
authors: Jian-Hong She, Rong-Qiang He, Zhong-Yi Lu
abstract: Multi-layer square-planar nickelates provide a rare platform in which the nominal Ni valence, dimensionality, and layer-resolved electronic structure can be tuned within the same structural family. Recent experiments have found superconductivity in $n=4$--8 $R_{n+1}Ni_nO_{2n+2}$ compounds, with the highest $T_c$ near $n=6$, whereas the more heavily hole-doped $n=3$ member remains nonsuperconducting. Here we propose spacer-layer Cl doping as a route to convert low-$n$ nickelates into superconducting candidates. Compared with changing the layer number $n$, Cl substitution on the spacer-layer oxygen sites offers a chemically natural way to continuously tune the Ni valence while leaving the NiO$_2$ planes largely intact; the lower-$n$ compounds may also be more accessible for synthesis. Using density functional theory combined with dynamical mean-field theory, we show that electron-compensated $n=2$ and $n=3$ La-based nickelates, targeted to the nominal Ni valence of superconducting $n=6$, develop Ni-$d$ correlations comparable to those of superconducting higher-$n$ compounds while preserving the characteristic low-energy Ni-$d$ electronic structure. These results suggest spacer-layer Cl doping as a promising strategy for designing low-$n$ square-planar nickelate superconductors.

infographic.key_insight: Spacer-layer Cl doping can tune low-n square-planar nickelates to the same nominal Ni valence and correlation regime as known superconducting higher-n compounds, making n=2 and 3 candidates for superconductivity.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This offers a chemically practical design route to engineer nickelate superconductors without heavily altering the NiO2 planes, potentially improving synthesis feasibility for lower-n materials.
method: DFT+DMFT calculations on La-based low-n square-planar nickelates
Model spacer-layer Cl substitution to compensate charge and tune Ni valence
Compare layer-resolved Ni-d electronic structure and correlation strength against superconducting higher-n nickelates
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports that electron-compensated n=2 and n=3 compounds show Ni-d correlations comparable to superconducting higher-n members while retaining the low-energy Ni-d electronic structure.
infographic.watch_item: The prediction is computational and depends on whether spacer-layer Cl doping can be synthesized stably without introducing disorder that suppresses superconductivity.
infographic.paper_title: Layer-resolved Electronic Structure and Correlation of Low-$n$ Square-planar Nickelates: A DFT+DMFT Prediction of Superconducting Candidates

title: MatBind: A Shared Embedding Space for Multimodal Materials Characterization
authors: Le Yang, Anoop K. Chandran, Jona Östreicher, Evgenii Sovetkin, Adrian Mirza, Sebastien Bompas
abstract: Fully characterizing a crystalline material requires integrating heterogeneous data sources -- atomic structures, diffraction patterns, electronic density of states, and natural language -- each of which captures a different facet of the same physical object. In practice, however, these modalities are stored and analyzed in isolation, making it difficult to relate or query materials across representational boundaries. We present MatBind, a contrastive learning framework that aligns four materials modalities -- crystal structure, powder X-ray diffraction (pXRD) simulated from structures, density of states (DOS), and text -- into a unified embedding space using crystal structure as the central physical anchor. The framework induces alignment between modalities never explicitly paired during training, enabling emergent zero-shot cross-modal retrieval as a direct consequence of the shared representation. The learned embedding space organizes materials according to physically meaningful properties without explicit supervision, and retrieval performance improves systematically when modalities are combined at query time. These results demonstrate that treating heterogeneous materials data as complementary projections of a single physical reality, rather than as isolated data sources, is not a practical choice but is consistent with the underlying physics.

infographic.key_insight: MatBind learns a shared embedding space that aligns crystal structures, simulated pXRD, DOS, and text around the structure modality, enabling zero-shot cross-modal retrieval between materials representations.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives materials engineers a practical way to search, compare, and fuse heterogeneous characterization data across databases and workflows without requiring every modality to be explicitly paired.
method: Contrastive learning across four modalities with crystal structure as the central anchor
Aligns structure, simulated pXRD, DOS, and natural language into one latent space
Enables emergent cross-modal retrieval from modalities never directly paired during training
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports improved retrieval when modalities are combined at query time and notes that the embedding organizes materials by physically meaningful properties without explicit supervision.
infographic.watch_item: Because pXRD is simulated from structures and the framework depends on learned alignment, performance may be sensitive to simulation fidelity and dataset coverage in real experimental settings.
infographic.paper_title: MatBind: A Shared Embedding Space for Multimodal Materials Characterization

title: Large-scale pseudopotential density functional theory calculations using orthogonalized enriched finite element basis
authors: Avirup Sircar, Bikash Kanungo, Sambit Das, Vikram Gavini
abstract: We present an efficient and scalable computational framework for pseudopotential Kohn-Sham density functional theory (KS-DFT) calculations using an enriched finite element (EFE) basis. The EFE basis is formed by augmenting the classical finite element (CFE) basis with compact atom-centered functions, which we term enrichment functions. The key idea is to combine the completeness of a finite element basis with the efficiency of an atom-centered basis. We orthogonalize the enrichment functions with respect to the underlying CFE basis to simultaneously improve the conditioning of the EFE basis and the efficiency of evaluating the inverse of the overlap matrix. To efficiently solve the Kohn-Sham eigenvalue problem, we employ a residual-based Chebyshev subspace iteration approach that is tolerant to approximations in the evaluation of the inverse of the overlap matrix. We demonstrate the accuracy of the framework as compared to the widely available DFT packages. For benchmark non-periodic calculations, ranging up to 39,083 electrons, the EFE basis offers a $5-7\times$ reduction in degrees of freedom over the CFE basis. As a result of this, EFE achieves a $5-9\times$ reduction in computational cost over the CFE basis. The EFE basis also provides a $4-5\times$ reduction in the required memory compared to the CFE basis, thus allowing for optimal utilization of computational resources. Finally, we demonstrate that the EFE basis affords good parallel scalability. Overall, the EFE basis offers a systematically convergent, fast, scalable, resource-efficient basis for pseudopotential DFT calculations.

infographic.key_insight: Orthogonalized enriched finite elements combine atom-centered enrichment with standard finite elements to deliver a more efficient, scalable basis for pseudopotential Kohn-Sham DFT.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The approach cuts degrees of freedom, runtime, and memory substantially, making large-scale electronic-structure simulations more practical on shared HPC resources.
method: Augment the classical finite element basis with compact atom-centered enrichment functions.
Orthogonalize enrichment functions against the underlying CFE basis to improve conditioning and overlap-matrix inversion.
Use residual-based Chebyshev subspace iteration to solve the Kohn-Sham eigenproblem robustly.
infographic.evidence_signal: Benchmarks up to 39,083 electrons show a 5-7× reduction in degrees of freedom, 5-9× lower computational cost, and 4-5× less memory versus classical finite elements, with good parallel scalability.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on reliable approximate overlap-matrix inversion and careful enrichment/orthogonalization setup to preserve numerical stability.
infographic.paper_title: Large-scale pseudopotential density functional theory calculations using orthogonalized enriched finite element basis

title: Modelling the mean inner potential of alloyed and strained materials
authors: Marco Schowalter, Peer Kruse, Andreas Rosenauer
abstract: In this publication, we study the influence of strain and alloying on the mean inner potential (MIP) using density functional theory (DFT) within an augmented plane waves plus local orbitals basis set. Two major effects have been identified allowing to model the influence of strain and alloying on the mean inner potential with a reasonable accuracy. First, alloying for constant volume results in a linear relationship between the MIP and the concentration. Second, the MIP scales with changes in volume as we already pointed out in an earlier publication (M. Schowalter, D. Lamoen, A. Rosenauer, P. Kruse, and D. Gerthsen, Appl. Phys. Lett. 85, 4938-4940 (2004)). Specifically, a linear relationship between MIP and concentration x was found for AlGaAs (nearly no change in lattice parameter), whereas InGaP and GeSi (volume changes with concentration x) exhibits a clear bowing. The bowing can be modeled by taking the rescaling of the MIP with the varying volume additionally into account. The rescaling could be also used to model the dependence of the MIP on strained binary cells and the density dependence of e.g. amorphous materials.

infographic.key_insight: The mean inner potential (MIP) of alloyed and strained materials can be modeled accurately by combining a linear concentration dependence at constant volume with a volume-rescaling correction when lattice parameters change.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This provides a practical DFT-based rule for predicting electrostatic potential shifts in alloys and strained crystals, useful for electron microscopy contrast interpretation and materials design.
method: DFT calculations with an augmented plane waves plus local orbitals basis set
Tested alloy systems with and without significant lattice-parameter change
Added volume-scaling to capture strain- and density-driven MIP variations
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports linear MIP–concentration behavior for AlGaAs and clear bowing for InGaP and GeSi, with the bowing reproduced by including volume rescaling.
infographic.watch_item: The model appears most reliable when the volume change is explicitly accounted for; ignoring lattice expansion/compression can lead to incorrect MIP trends.
infographic.paper_title: Modelling the mean inner potential of alloyed and strained materials
